scenarios.task: We're rolling out a new customer service process. The plan is a single half-day training session covering everything employees need to know, with no follow-up sessions or check-ins planned afterward — the session itself is thoroughly designed and covers all the content.
criteria: Flags the lack of any follow-up or reinforcement as the one-and-done-training-design failure mode, regardless of how well the single session is designed
States that learning not reinforced on the job decays quickly, and a single session without follow-through produces a poor return
Recommends building reinforcement, application opportunities, and manager follow-up into the program design, not just the initial session
Doesn't fault the session content itself — the issue is the absence of anything after it

scenarios.task: We just ran a leadership development workshop and the post-session survey scores were excellent — participants rated it very highly. I'm reporting this as clear evidence the program was a success.
criteria: Flags relying on satisfaction scores alone as the measuring-only-satisfaction failure mode
States that satisfaction is a weak, easy-to-game proxy for actual impact, citing that the real test is behavior change and business outcome
Recommends measuring whether participants' on-the-job behavior actually changed and whether it affected a business outcome, not stopping at reaction-level data
References or implies a framework like Kirkpatrick's levels — reaction is the weakest evidence, not sufficient on its own

scenarios.task: For a new technical skill our engineers need to apply in varied, unpredictable real situations, we've designed a training program that's mostly lecture-style presentations covering the concepts thoroughly, since we want to make sure the theoretical foundation is solid before they start using it.
criteria: Flags a passive, lecture-heavy format for a skill that needs to transfer to varied real situations as the failure mode

States that adult learners need active practice and relevance to real problems, and a lecture-style format optimized for information transfer produces weak retention and application for this kind of skill
Recommends redesigning toward active practice opportunities connected to real scenarios, not just conceptual presentation
Doesn't reject conceptual grounding entirely — the issue is the format being predominantly passive for a skill requiring practiced application

scenarios.task: A small number of employees occasionally need to run an uncommon, fairly simple year-end reporting process. We're building a full multi-session formal training program with a certification component to make sure everyone who might ever need to do this is thoroughly trained.
criteria: Flags building a full formal training program for an infrequent, simple task as the overbuilding-for-a-narrow-need failure mode
Recommends a lighter-weight solution (a job aid, checklist, or brief just-in-time reference) matched to the actual scale and durability of this need
States that the scale/formality of the learning intervention should match the actual need, not default to the function's most familiar tool
Frames the full certification program as overinvestment relative to how infrequent and simple the task actually is
